ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated congee node; A00LC (rice-based porridge) is the closest defensible parent under the cereal porridge hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chinese rice porridge (congee) is approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as commonly served in Singapore hawker stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Rice porridge (congee), plain — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain rice porridge (congee) entry from HPB Singapore Foodo database used as primary source. Values represent plain white-rice congee without toppings, consistent with the base dish served at Hai Kah Lang and similar Singaporean Chinese porridge stalls.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Congee (rice porridge)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice congee (plain white rice porridge) has a published GI of approximately 78 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (78 × 9.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 22.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Plain rice porridge contains approximately 120 mg sodium per 100 g (360 mg per standard 300 g bowl); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid high-sodium toppings such as preserved vegetables, salted fish, or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 78) due to extensively hydrated and gelatinised starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice porridge has a high GI of 78 and a glycemic load of approximately 22.5 per standard bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with high-protein or high-fibre toppings (e.g. lean fish, egg, vegetables) to blunt postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain rice porridge is very low in total fat (0.1 g/100 g) and saturated fat, making it a heart-friendly base dish; however, cardiovascular risk increases substantially when high-fat or high-sodium toppings such as pork offal, fried dough, or preserved meats are added.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese rice porridge, known as congee (粥, zhōu), is a traditional staple dish made by simmering white rice in a large volume of water until the grains fully break down into a smooth, thick, creamy gruel. It is one of the most widely consumed comfort foods across Chinese communities in Singapore, China, Hong Kong, and Southeast Asia. The texture is soft and silky, with a mild, neutral flavour that serves as a versatile base for a wide range of savoury toppings including century egg, salted egg, minced pork, fish slices, chicken, pork offal, fried shallots, ginger, and spring onion. In Singapore, Chinese porridge is a hawker staple available at breakfast, lunch, and dinner, and is particularly popular as a light meal for the elderly, the unwell, or those seeking easily digestible food. Nutritionally, plain congee is low in energy (approximately 44 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat, and moderate in carbohydrate (9.6 g/100 g), but has a high glycemic index (GI 78) due to the extensive gelatinisation of starch during prolonged cooking. This makes portion control and topping selection clinically important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate in plain preparations but can rise significantly with seasoning and toppings. Congee is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets in its plain form.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of Chinese porridge?
Is rice porridge good for diabetics?
What is the GI of congee?
How much sodium is in Chinese porridge?
What toppings are served with congee in Singapore?
Is congee the same as rice porridge?

confidence_reason: Plain rice porridge (congee) is an exact match in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. Nutrient values are drawn directly from the HPB primary source. GI value of 78 is published in the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean Chinese dish are present.
notes: Nutrient values represent plain white-rice congee without toppings. The restaurant listed (Hai Kah Lang, Funan Mall) is a Teochew-style seafood porridge stall; the base porridge composition is consistent with plain congee values from HPB. Saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for plain congee; fat content is negligible in plain rice porridge with no added oil. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; no hydrogenated fats are used in plain congee preparation. Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; plain congee contains no added or intrinsic sugars. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for plain rice porridge with no animal-derived toppings. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; white rice contains no vitamin C. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for plain congee. GrabFood source URL and restaurant metadata preserved from input for traceability. Price range SGD 4.82 noted from input but not used as a nutritional evidence source.
